item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why didn't the secret service just leave the snakestone in space…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, cool, glad this got featured. I've been quite proud of this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, damn that is brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With the most recent Supermoon…

Obama: Glad you're here, because there's one more thing you need to be aware of.

Trump: What's that? Nice getup with the Special Forces.

Extradimensional gate opens and Special Forces open fire on the emerging basaltic ophidioid

Obama: All the goddamn moon snakes.

Trump: …

Biden: …I was hoping we could keep the moon snakes a surprise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea, and the concept of GRU-P turning a harmless ritualistic anomaly into a security threat of such magnitude.

Headcanon: Somewhere, there's a gallery of photos of each president and their first kill.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, think about it. Obama being chasing by a large stone snake for every month, in the white house.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, it's so over the top silly (giant snakes… from the moon!) while still making sense in-universe. That last line is absolute excellent too, great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this one; I've always liked organizations modifying anomalies for some purpose, and the silliness of the treaties and accidents involves feels all too real considering the ineptitudes of some bureaucracies. Not to mention, well, a 60 foot snake would probably present quite an issue for the president, so points for ideas there to GRU-P :p

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get why so many people complain about "stereotyping" in this skip. Is it truly inconceivable that a Cold War era, Soviet, paranormal scientist would still harbor some deep-seated hatred towards the US? Don't you think this would come up in an interview of his sordid past, when he designed a weapon to kill the president? If anything, Vetrov seems a bit too Americanized.

As to the GRU torturing the Mexican revolutionaries, that's what they do, they're pretty ruthless. The only reason Vetrov thinks the foundation doesn't do that kind of stuff is because he doesn't know about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh holy hell. This is amazing. +1.

By the way - it says that it attacks at solar midnight during a full moon for the target. I.e., the President.

Solution: send the President to the Moon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ending wins it for me, again

"Simply put, today I realize that if the President were eaten by a giant snake from the moon, it would just be more trouble than it’s worth."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It makes me think of Graham Chapman every time someone posts that.

I like a little silly with my breakfast. The steerotyping really didn't hurt it for me; I've seen interviews with cold-war Russian rocket scientists that aren't that far off tonewise. As mentioned upstream, it's a catastrophic sequence of errors, each of which is believable. I can see the Secret Service, after consulting with defense scientists, launching this thing. I'm sure there was a plan for recovery with Apollo 19…oh, that got cancelled? Hmm.

+1 for a morning grin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1. Too silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe they could get the moon wizard to help find it. MOON WIZARD

…

Anyhow, I like this. It's a convoluted history of a formerly innocuous object that, after changing hands and plans many times, is now slowly excreting the moon onto the Earth in the form of giant snakes trying to kill the president.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simply put, today I realize that if the President were eaten by a giant snake from the moon, it would just be more trouble than it’s worth.

I wish I had enough upvotes to give this for all the snakes on the moon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this concept. Moon snakes attacking the President? Never thought I'd upvote that. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Snake didn't (try to) kill the president, that was Raiden.

but seriously this idea is incredible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not to debate politics too much but while I like the concept of this one, huge chunks of the article just read like cardboard cutout Russians during the Soviet era as imagined by yanks parts of it are the kind of thing you'd expect to read in a US cold-war propaganda novel. I see very little reason to think that GRU would be more likely to gratuitously torture Mexican revolutionaries than their foundation equivalents- and that's just one example in the article. The bit where the two conversants comment on how nice it is that the foundation doesn't torture people unnecessarily like GRU did is nauseating.

Also I don't think the US government would be stupid enough to put the damn thing on the moon without a backup plan.

So no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation has a portal to the moon, but it's to the wrong part of the moon, it's one-way, and it's rather impractical. Other than that, we don't really have much of an indication of them having a space program. With good reason. Space programs are titanically expensive by any standard, impossible to do in secret, and would offer the Foundation very little benefit. They're not going to do something so organizationally crippling without very good reason, and this SCP isn't enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, pleased to see this is on the Russian wiki now. Thanks, GeneR.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fucking fantastic, but I gotta say the first sentence feels wrong to me- the Foundation could go to the moon for a fucking weekend break, resource expenditures are hardly a concern. If one thing could perfect this it would be changing the emphasis in the containment procedures from the resources required to the difficulty in maintaining secrecy while retrieving it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that we actually know how this thing was made (or modified, rather) and got to interview one of its creators. That was a nice touch.

item.title: Dat final sentence.
item.content:encoded:
Let's see:

Indestructible. Sphere. That can never again be contained. What makes other SCPs.

And yet, it still got my upvote, because the story makes it work. Well done.

…that, and the final sentence of the addendum almost made me spew coffee in my keyboard, without taking away from the general creepiness.
This is utterly brilliant and hits my weird sense quite nicely. Enjoy your +50.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So glad this finally got posted. I forget how you phrased it in the chat, but something along the lines of "you start out with the best of intentions, and the next thing you know stone snakes from the moon are trying to eat the President". I love it.

This is how you do things getting out of hand. Taken piece by piece, none of the individual actions in this story are horrible ideas per se. Develop a weapon that can be used to target high-level individuals? Sure, why not. Put this indestructible thing on the moon because it uses moon phases to activate? Hey, worth a shot. But through a series of bureaucracies doing things with an item they don't quite understand, you're now stuck with moon snakes attacking the President every month. It's absolutely ridiculous, but it's got enough straight men in the story to not be stupid.

I also liked the interview. The friendly, informal meeting with a POI that's been cultivated as an intelligence source is a welcome change of pace from ye olde standard intake interview in a bunker somewhere.

Overall, very nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really neat and has all kinds of implications. The last line of interview really sold it for me (up until then, it was a bit "Here's how everything happened!" and "Defeat evil Western empire!" though I see your comments on that).

I, too, would like to see the paragraphs spaced out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The GRU can manipulate an object's properties if they have the object on hand. Boris isn't in containment for much the same reason as every Prometheus lab tech isn't — knowledge of the supernatural is widespread enough that the Foundation doesn't want to waste time and resources trying to lock up everyone who has ever known anything about anomalies (Boris explicitly hasn't been active for decades), and if Boris is being as cooperative as he is, locking him up FOREVUR would just discourage other people from helping you in the future. We're concealing it these days by just having a large underground bunker where they're able to pretty easily dispatch the snake. Earlier on, yeah, that's a bit of a logical hole.

As for your last point, I mean, that's sort of the way we've set up the universe, isn't it? The Foundation are hoarders, not users. If it's core to the Foundation's identity that they try to prevent the spread of anomalies, and core to the GRU-P that they make anomalous Cold War superweapons, well, it's kind of justifiable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm staying neutral on this one. The anomaly is solid and inventive, and the background almost makes sense, but some of the details strain credulity.

  • If it's so easy for the GRU-P to manipulate an object's anomalous properties, why can't the Foundation do it to go retrieve the object?
  • If this Boris is known to have done this in the past, why is he not in containment?
  • How on earth are we hiding the emergence of a 60 foot long rock-serpent every 28 days? Seems to me the White House would have needed at least 3 renovations before they discovered the pattern.

I dunno man… On one hand you have all the other organizations who seem to be able to make and unmake anomalous objects at whim, and then there's the stodgy Foundation, which is either too thick-headed to learn anything after decades of research or too stubborn to use what it has learned to the betterment of its mission.

…All the same, I like the object, so I don't want to see it disappear.

0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I guess it's just a matter of resources then. My headcanon leans further along the 'unfathomably huge' level of Foundation money/personnel/resources than the leaner, meaner Foundation that's gotten more prevalent over time, but the article makes more sense along the lean side.

I didn't pick up on the bit about it having buried itself underground, though. That solves the 'take pictures of the moon' solution, and does help a lot towards reconciling the irretrievable aspect in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
10 meter crater? That's tiny. And it's already buried far underground, because it's been doing this for decades. "Watch the moon" isn't so easy, and wouldn't even do the trick. It's not impossible, and actually in one regard easier than you suppose since we know about where the US dropped it off. But locating it precisely, getting there, extracting it, and then somehow getting it back to Earth is not an easy task to achieve one 37.8 kg load at a time.

It would probably be easier to launch a bunch of probes to the moon to carefully triangulate the almost imperceptible seismic effect of an activation event, then send a mission there to mine it up and bring it back to Earth. But this is still a phenomenal resource expenditure, and not something the Foundation can do itself without blowing secrecy.

It's not impossible to recover, but the Foundation is not well-equipped to do so, moon base or no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I missed something about the artifact relocating itself after every event. Otherwise, it doesn't seem like it'd be that hard to watch the moon from a satellite or something around the scheduled time for an event, then go scour the place where a new 10-meter crater suddenly appeared. Or a half of the moon, or a quarter…if the Secret Service has the 2762-1's in hand, the Foundation can take a few months for surveillance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, SCP-120 states that there's a Foundation moonbase (although some people don't have that as part of their headcanon), but I think you're not quite getting the scope involved.

As a comparison, imagine that you've been tasked with finding a suitcase nuke located somewhere in Asia, and that's literally all the info you have on it. How fast would you be able to find it, and how much effort would it take?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They do have a moonbase. But it's not exactly near this thing, finding it isn't easy, and getting it back to Earth is also a challenge. It would probably be easier to get to this thing's location from Earth than from the Foundation moonbase, simply because you would have so overwhelmingly more resources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amusing enough to upvote.

But doesn't the Foundation have a moon base? Why can't they just go and hunt it down?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anaconda-ve said it better myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't intended to be much of a mystery. The joke is how it got there, step by step. Could this also work as just a mysterious object without any backstory? Yeah, probably. But that's not the story I wanted to tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idunno… I feel like the interview log explains too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn it, man, moon snakes trying to eat the president for the communist cause should not be a good SCP. And yet it is. I applaud you and upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yesss, I can finally upvote it. This is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't mean to rattler anybody, I just wanted to snake in here and comment on the scale of this endeavor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stop it you copperheaded cottonmouth.

yes this makes sense don't question it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it could get much badder than that.

see it's like an adder like that asmodeus poisonteeth from that one redwall book, get it do you get it aren't i just the cleverest

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nonhazardous is clearer. I'll change that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What an aboamination of a pun.

Is there room in the corner for two?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Communist Mesoamerican moon snakes are trying to kill the President! Are you a bad enough dude to stop them?!"

This is very silly in all the right ways. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to point out that in the third paragraph of Special Containment Procedures you said that the container should be filled with "water or some other nonreactive liquid," which doesn't make sense considering that water reacts with a whole lot of things. Maybe instead you could use "water or some other nonlethal liquid."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs some spaces between lines in the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't you mean, "in the asp?"

…

I'm so sorry please I'll go sit in the corner now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still don't know how I feel about these micro-canon skips. I guess since the list is getting watered down we need to make small universes to make the objects look interesting in their own context. Whatever works.

Simply put, today I realize that if the President were eaten by a giant snake from the moon, it would just be more trouble than it’s worth. Good day to you, Doctor.

This sold me because of the image of the shattered remains of a giant snake over the ruins of DC flooded with cheese.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do not moon the snakes. That kind of thing may come back to bite you in the ass.
